Scientific Computing for Physics

A modern introduction to Scientific Computing for Physics and Engineering students using Python.

Welcome to Scientific Computing for Physics, a long-term educational project that integrates scientific programming, data analysis, visualization, numerical methods, and Physics into a single learning path.

The goal of this project is to show that programming should not be learned separately from Physics, but rather as a tool for understanding physical phenomena, building computational models, and analyzing results.

This material is continuously developed and is intended to evolve over time into a complete resource for undergraduate students, educators, and researchers.

Objectives

This project aims to:

  • Introduce Scientific Computing using Python.
  • Teach programming applied to Physics.
  • Develop skills in scientific data analysis and visualization.
  • Present numerical methods gradually and in context.
  • Complement traditional Physics teaching with computational experiments.
  • Provide an open and free educational resource.

Project Organization

The content is organized into four major parts.

Part I — Scientific Computing

Introduction to the fundamental tools used throughout the entire project.

Topics include:

  • Python
  • Jupyter Notebooks
  • NumPy
  • Pandas
  • Matplotlib
  • SciPy
  • Scientific project organization

Part II — Physics and Data Science

Introduction to the fundamental concepts of scientific data analysis.

The notebooks in this section cover topics such as:

  • experimental data analysis;
  • scientific visualization;
  • descriptive statistics;
  • regression;
  • least squares;
  • experimental uncertainties;
  • scientific communication.

Part III — Physics

Most of the project is dedicated to the traditional undergraduate Physics curriculum.

The content approximately follows the sequence of a university course:

  • Physics I — Mechanics
  • Physics II — Waves, Fluids, and Thermodynamics
  • Physics III — Electromagnetism
  • Physics IV — Optics and Modern Physics

Each notebook combines theory, computational implementation, visualizations, simulations, and exercises.

Part IV — Numerical Methods

The algorithms used throughout the project are gathered in this section, where they are studied in greater depth.

Planned topics include:

  • numerical differentiation;
  • numerical integration;
  • root finding;
  • interpolation;
  • linear systems;
  • ordinary differential equations;
  • partial differential equations;
  • Monte Carlo methods;
  • optimization.

Target Audience

This project was developed primarily for:

  • undergraduate Physics students;
  • Engineering students;
  • educators;
  • researchers beginning in Scientific Computing;
  • anyone interested in learning Physics using Python.

Technologies

All content is developed using free and open-source tools.

  • Python
  • Jupyter Notebook
  • NumPy
  • Pandas
  • Matplotlib
  • SciPy
  • Quarto

Continuous Development

This is a long-term project.

New notebooks, examples, simulations, and animations will be added continuously, covering the main topics of undergraduate Physics and Engineering.

The goal is to build, over time, a complete educational resource in Scientific Computing for Physics.

License

This project is released under the MIT License.
